title: Quiz: Classical Optimizers for Quantum Circuits

questions.question: What is the main advantage of SPSA (Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation) over finite-difference gradient methods for quantum optimization?

options.text: SPSA produces exact gradients rather than approximations
SPSA requires only 2 function evaluations per iteration regardless of parameter count
SPSA converges to the global optimum guaranteed
SPSA eliminates the need for hyperparameter tuning

questions.explanation: SPSA requires only 2 function evaluations per iteration (one at +perturbation and one at -perturbation) regardless of the number of parameters. In contrast, finite-difference methods require 2n evaluations where n is the number of parameters. This makes SPSA highly efficient for high-dimensional quantum optimization problems.

questions.question: In the parameter shift rule for computing quantum gradients, what shift value is typically used for standard rotation gates (RX, RY, RZ)?

options.text: A small value like 0.001
Exactly 1.0
pi/2 (90 degrees)
2*pi (full rotation)

questions.explanation: The parameter shift rule uses a shift of pi/2 for standard rotation gates. The gradient formula is: df/dtheta = [f(theta + pi/2) - f(theta - pi/2)] / (2 * sin(pi/2)) = [f(theta + pi/2) - f(theta - pi/2)] / 2. This gives exact gradients for parameterized quantum circuits with rotation gates.

questions.question: Which optimizer would be most appropriate for a VQE problem with 100+ parameters and significant measurement noise?

options.text: Nelder-Mead simplex method
L-BFGS-B
SPSA
Newton's method with exact Hessian

questions.explanation: SPSA is ideal for this scenario because: (1) it handles high noise well through stochastic gradient estimation, (2) it scales efficiently to many parameters (only 2 evaluations per iteration), and (3) it has proven convergence for noisy objectives. Nelder-Mead doesn't scale well, L-BFGS-B and Newton's method are sensitive to noise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'patience' parameter in early stopping for quantum optimization?

options.text: To set the maximum number of circuit executions
To define how many iterations without improvement before stopping
To control the learning rate decay schedule
To set the minimum number of shots per measurement

questions.explanation: The patience parameter defines how many consecutive iterations without meaningful improvement (cost reduction below min_delta) the optimizer will tolerate before stopping. This prevents wasting computational resources when the optimization has converged or is stuck in a local minimum.

questions.question: In the Adam optimizer, what is the purpose of bias correction for the moment estimates?

options.text: To prevent overflow in floating-point calculations
To compensate for initialization bias toward zero in early iterations
To add regularization and prevent overfitting
To ensure the optimizer respects parameter bounds

questions.explanation: Adam initializes both moment estimates (m and v) to zero. In early iterations, this causes the moment estimates to be biased toward zero. Bias correction (dividing by 1 - beta^t) compensates for this, ensuring that the effective learning rate is appropriate from the first iteration.

questions.question: Natural gradient descent differs from standard gradient descent by:

options.text: Using random perturbations instead of computed gradients
Incorporating the geometry of parameter space via the Fisher Information Matrix
Only updating one parameter at a time
Always converging to the global minimum

questions.explanation: Natural gradient descent uses the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM) to account for the geometry of the parameter space. The update rule is theta_new = theta - eta * F^(-1) * gradient, where F is the QFIM. This leads to more efficient optimization as it properly weighs updates based on how sensitively the quantum state changes with each parameter.

questions.question: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) requires gradient information to optimize quantum circuits.
questions.explanation: False. COBYLA is a gradient-free optimization method. It uses linear approximations of the objective function constructed from function evaluations only. This makes it suitable for quantum optimization where gradient computation may be expensive or noisy. COBYLA is also one of the few gradient-free methods that naturally handles constraints.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid strategies for handling noisy objective functions in quantum optimization? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Averaging multiple evaluations at each point
Using larger perturbation sizes in gradient estimation
Implementing multiple random restarts
Reducing the learning rate as noise increases
Ignoring the noise since optimizers handle it automatically

questions.explanation: All strategies except (e) are valid: (a) Averaging reduces noise variance by a factor of sqrt(n). (b) Larger perturbations make the gradient signal stronger relative to noise. (c) Multiple restarts help escape local minima and find better solutions. (d) Smaller learning rates prevent overshooting due to noisy gradient estimates. Noise does NOT automatically get handled and requires explicit strategies.
